title: Chapter 29: Expedition Prep
content: Day 22. Twenty-one hours until departure.
The fortress hummed with the focused energy of a community preparing for war.

Marcus stood in the garage bay—a section of the ground floor that Rebecca's engineers had converted into a workshop complete with welding stations, tool racks, and the kind of organized chaos that meant people who knew what they were doing—watching the SUV undergo its transformation.

The Armored Transport Upgrade Kit had materialized as a shimmering blueprint in his inventory: reinforced titanium plating for doors and undercarriage, puncture-resistant run-flat tires, a ram bar welded to the front bumper strong enough to push through a stalled bus, and a roof-mounted frequency emitter that could broadcast Sera's counter-frequency in a fifty-meter radius around the vehicle. Not enough to clear a permanent zone, but enough to create a moving bubble of relative safety through hostile territory.

"Thirty millimeter titanium alloy on the doors," said Raj, one of Rebecca's engineers—a compact man with callused hands and the methodical patience of someone who'd spent his pre-apocalypse life fixing things that shouldn't have been fixable. "It'll stop small-arms fire and most melee impacts. Won't stop a .50 cal, but if Victor Crane's boys are shooting .50 cals at you, you've got bigger problems than armor."
"Fuel?" Marcus asked.

"Full tank. The System's generator provides a slow charge to the hybrid battery—indefinite range at low speeds, maybe six hours of hard driving before you need to stop." Raj tightened a bolt on the ram bar. "I'd still pack jerrycans. The apocalypse is full of 'maybes' that turn into 'definitely nots.'"

Elena appeared with her field kit—a military-grade backpack the System had provided at Level 8 and that she'd since loaded with trauma supplies, IV kits, emergency medications, and three days' worth of ration bars. She set it by the SUV's open trunk and started inventory-checking against her clipboard.
"Sternal needle, chest seal, tourniquets x4, hemostatic gauze—eight packets—" She murmured the items like a prayer, each one a lifeline she might need to throw in the next forty-eight hours.
Marcus left her to it. There were other preparations, and they didn't stop.

In the armory, Sera was running through weapons checks with the quiet efficiency of someone who'd never needed a weapon before she died and now couldn't afford to be without one. Two combat knives, custom-forged from System-provided alloys. A reinforced baton for close-quarters work where killing raiders would be counterproductive. And the Shadow Weave—her evolved defensive carapace, stored now as a compact module that fused with her body on activation.

She moved through the forms with a dancer's grace and a predator's focus, each strike precise, each pivot balanced, each breath measured to the half-second. She'd been practicing for weeks, teaching her own body how to fight in a shell that was stronger and faster than the one she'd been born with.

Marcus watched from the doorway. He'd seen her fight before—the Alpha, the warehouse horde, the ferals that occasionally tested the perimeter—but watching her *train* was different. This was deliberate. Controlled. The martial equivalent of a CEO reviewing quarterly projections, identifying weaknesses, hardening soft spots.
She finished with a spinning kick that cracked the training dummy's reinforced spine and turned to find him leaning against the frame.
"Staring," she said.

"Appreciating." He held up the inventory tablet. "Supply check. We've got water for four days, rations for three, medical supplies for two trauma cases, and one very ambitious nursing student who thinks she's invincible."

"She's not wrong about being needed." Sera toweled off—she still produced a light sheen of perspiration, one of the many uncanny-valley details of Stage 4 evolution. More human than a zombie, less human than a living person. "Lily will need immediate medical assessment. Panic room survival isn't just about oxygen—carbon dioxide buildup, dehydration, potential pressure injuries from prolonged confinement in a sealed space."
"You've been researching."

"I was CEO of Vale Industries. I had the panic rooms installed." Her voice flattened. "I know exactly what they're rated for and exactly what happens when those ratings are exceeded. The fact that Lily's still alive after twenty-two days means she found a way to extend the air supply—probably by reducing her activity to near-zero, staying as still as possible, breathing shallowly." Sera's jaw tightened. "My sister has been lying motionless in the dark for three weeks, trying not to use too much air. She's probably aware that every breath might be her last."
The words hung in the air between them—horrifying, unavoidable, the kind of knowledge that demanded action and punished delay.
Marcus crossed the armory and took her hand. "Tomorrow. She won't be alone anymore after tomorrow."
Sera nodded, but her silver eyes stayed distant—fixed on a point in time that hadn't happened yet, a door she hadn't opened, a sister she hadn't saved.
The afternoon blurred into a montage of last-minute preparations.

Marcus programmed the route into the SUV's nav system: six miles from New Eden to Vale Industries Tower, threading through the least-dense zombie zones, avoiding the major intersections where Crane's raiders had set up checkpoints, skirting the industrial district entirely. Two estimated travel hours each way, assuming no major encounters. Four hours on-site for extraction and assessment. Twelve hours total with contingency buffer.

Raj finished the SUV armor and moved on to installing the frequency emitter on the roof—a delicate operation that required calibrating the resonance to match Sera's exact counter-frequency signature. "One decibel off and it'll attract zombies instead of repelling them," he warned. "No pressure."

Elena briefed the backup medical team—two survivors with basic first-aid training who'd hold down the fortress infirmary in her absence. She walked them through triage protocols, medication schedules, and the location of every supply in the medical bay with the thoroughness of someone who'd accepted that she might not come back.

Rebecca sent a reconnaissance update via hand radio: Crane's raiders had increased patrols in the downtown corridor, possibly in response to the counter-frequency expansion. They'd spotted at least four checkpoints along the most direct route to Vale Industries Tower. The indirect route added forty minutes but bypassed three of them.

Sera reconfigured the relay network from the war room, extending the safe zone's coverage to compensate for her absence. The Evolved guards would hold the perimeter—they'd proven reliable in her previous solo excursions—but the counter-frequency would be weaker without her direct projection. She compensated by overclocking the Alpha Heart Crystal amplifier, squeezing an extra fifteen percent range out of the system at the cost of higher power consumption.
"Three weeks," she told Marcus, showing him the power curve. "We have three weeks before the crystal needs replacement. More than enough for a two-day expedition."

"More than enough," he agreed, and didn't mention the other calculation running through his head: if they failed, if the expedition went sideways, if Crane caught them, the crystal's lifespan wouldn't matter because there wouldn't be anyone left to use it.
Night fell over New Eden like a held breath.

The fortress settled into the quiet hours—generator humming, relays pulsing, the perimeter guards' footsteps a distant rhythm on the walls. Elena had gone to sleep early, her field kit packed and waiting by the door. Raj and the engineers had retreated to the Parkview contingent's quarters, their work done. The SUV sat in the garage bay, transformed from a sturdy off-road vehicle into something that might survive downtown.
Marcus found Sera on the roof.

She was sitting on the edge—feet dangling, silver eyes fixed on the city skyline, the distant silhouette of Vale Industries Tower a dark needle against the star-scattered sky. The tower was too far to see clearly, but she knew where it was. She could *feel* it now—the faint thread connecting her to Lily, pulsing like a low-grade headache at the edge of her awareness.
He sat beside her. The night was cool—late autumn settling into the bones of the world, another season the apocalypse had stolen the meaning from.
For a long time, neither of them spoke. The Bond said everything words couldn't: his determination, her fear, the shared weight of a promise that might be impossible to keep.
Then Sera said, very quietly: "What if I'm already a monster?"

Marcus turned. She wasn't looking at him—she was looking at her hands, which in the moonlight looked almost human, if you ignored the faint silvery sheen of evolved skin and the too-smooth texture of fingers that had once been cold and dead.
"What do you mean?"

"Twenty-two days. Lily has been in that panic room for twenty-two days, and I've been here—building, organizing, *thriving*." Sera's voice cracked at the last word. "I felt her signal this morning and my first thought wasn't terror. It was *good, now we know*. Like she was intel instead of my sister. Like she was a *quest objective*."
"She's not—"

"Isn't she?" Sera's silver eyes finally met his, and in them he saw something he'd never seen before in her: self-disgust. "The System categorized her as C008. A target. A reward. And some part of me—some part of what I've *become*—responded to that categorization like it was correct. Like she's not my sister anymore, she's a *resource*."
The words fell into the silence between them, heavy and terrible.

Marcus didn't respond immediately. Through the Bond, he let himself feel the full scope of her fear—not just the fear of losing Lily, but the fear of what she was becoming. A Stage 4 evolved zombie who talked like a human and loved like a human and *mostly* felt like a human, but whose instincts had been rewritten by a virus that didn't care about sisterhood or love or the things that made people people.
"Come here," he said.

Sera hesitated. Then she shifted, closing the distance between them, and Marcus wrapped his arms around her and pulled her against his chest. She was warm—she was always warm now, the Eternal Bond maintaining her body temperature at something close to human—and she fit against him like she'd been designed for it, because in a way, she had been.

"You're not a monster," he said into her hair. "You're a woman who's been put through hell and come out the other side still capable of caring about someone else. A monster wouldn't be *afraid* of being a monster, Sera. A monster wouldn't care that she had the wrong thought. The fact that you're horrified by your own reaction—*that's* the proof. That's the part of you that's still you."

"What if the horror is programmed too?" Her voice was muffled against his chest. "What if the System made me feel guilty on purpose, because guilt keeps the Bond stable, because a guilty zombie queen is a *useful* zombie queen—"

"Then the System is a better romance engine than I gave it credit for," Marcus said, "because *I'm* telling you you're not a monster, and I'm not programmed. I'm just a guy who loves you and sees you clearly."

She went still. Through the Bond, he felt the wall she'd been holding up—the wall of CEO composure, zombie efficiency, the ruthless practicality that had kept her functional through three weeks of not knowing if her sister was alive—crack along its length.

"I've been so *scared*," she whispered. "Every day I don't feel her, I think she's dead, and every day I *do* feel something—like this morning—I think it's false hope, or worse, that I'm just *projecting*, that I want to feel her so badly that I'm manufacturing the signal—"

"You weren't manufacturing it. The System confirmed her vitals. She's alive, Sera. She's *alive*, and she's waiting, and tomorrow we're going to walk through hell if we have to because that's what we do."

Sera pulled back just enough to look at him, and her silver eyes were bright with something that might have been tears if Stage 4 zombies could cry. They couldn't—not yet—but the emotion behind the gesture was real, and through the Bond it hit Marcus like a wave.

"I love you," she said. Not casual this time. Not the comfortable, everyday version they exchanged over breakfast. This was the raw, desperate version—the kind of love that existed at the edge of loss, where everything was fragile and precious and temporary.

"I love you too." He cupped her face in his hands. "And tomorrow, we're going to give Lily the chance to love you too. She's going to meet you, and she's going to see what I see—a woman who came back from death to protect the people she cares about."
"What if she's afraid of me? What if she sees me and sees a monster?"
"Then we'll show her you're not. Together. As many times as it takes."

Sera leaned into his palms, and the Bond between them flared—not with quest notifications or affection points, but with something simpler. *Trust*. The deep, irrational trust of two people who'd chosen each other in the worst possible circumstances and kept choosing, day after day, because the alternative was being alone.
**[SERAPHINA VALE — AFFECTION: 78 → 80/100]**

**[Eternal Bond Stability: REINFORCED]**
**[Bond Resonance: Deepened — Emotional vulnerability shared, trust reaffirmed]**
**[Note: The strongest bonds are forged not in certainty, but in the courage to show doubt.]**

Marcus held her until her trembling stopped, until the Bond settled from a fever-pitch to a warm hum, until the stars wheeled overhead and the distant pulse of Lily's fading heartbeat reminded them both why they were sitting on this roof at midnight instead of sleeping.
When they finally went inside, Sera paused at the doorway and looked back at the city.
"Dawn," she said.
"Dawn," Marcus agreed.

And somewhere in the dark needle of Vale Industries Tower, surrounded by raiders and silence and the slow dread of running out of air, Lily Vale took another shallow breath and held it—as she'd been holding it for twenty-two days—waiting for a sister she didn't know was coming.
She wouldn't have to wait much longer.
